incorrects.description: Student selected A: misidentified equal groups as unequal

steps.text: Look closely at each bag in A. Count what's inside each one. Do they all have the same number?

incorrects.description: Student selected B: misidentified equal groups as unequal

steps.text: Check each circle in B. Count the dots inside. Are the amounts different, or are they all the same?

incorrects.description: Student selected D: misidentified equal groups as unequal

steps.text: Look at D. Count what's in each bag. Does every bag have the same amount inside?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Option C highlights. System demonstrates by showing the unequal amounts.

steps.text: Let me show you. For groups to be equal, every container needs the same number inside. In C, one box has 3 items and the other has 5. Those amounts are different, so C is not equal groups. The answer is C.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5 groups of 3: reversed the number of groups and size of groups

steps.text: Count how many containers you see in each picture. Bags, circles, symbols. Then check how many items are inside each one.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 + 5: interpreted as addition instead of groups

steps.text: Addition means we combine different amounts. Here, every container holds the same amount. That's groups, not addition. How many groups? How many in each group?

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 things with no groups: saw total but missed the group structure

steps.text: Yes, there are 15 total. But look at how they're organized. Items are sorted into containers. Each container holds the same amount. That's what makes them groups.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System counts the 3 bags one by one
System counts 5 items in the first bag

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the containers: 3 bags. I count what's in one bag: 5 items. Same pattern in the circles: 3 circles, 5 dots each. Same in the graph: 3 symbols, each symbol equals 5. All three pictures show 3 groups of 5. That's the structure multiplication describes.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of containers on first attempt

steps.text: Check the scenario. How many packs are on the shelf?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of containers on second attempt

params.description: The phrase '4 packs' highlights in the scenario text.

steps.text: The scenario says 4 packs of juice boxes. Each pack is one group. How many groups do you need?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of containers three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets the container count to 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. The scenario says 4 packs of juice boxes. Each pack is one group, so we need 4 containers. When we see packs, we count how many packs there are. That tells us how many groups.

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of items per container on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the scenario again. How many juice boxes are in each pack?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of items per container on second attempt

params.description: The phrase '6 juice boxes' highlights in the scenario text.

steps.text: The scenario says each pack has 6 juice boxes. That means 6 items go in each container. How many items per container?

incorrects.description: Student set wrong number of items per container three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets 6 items in each container.

steps.text: Here's how it works. The scenario says each pack has 6 juice boxes. That 6 is the number that goes in every container. When we see 'each pack has 6,' that tells us the size of each group. The answer is 6 items per container because every pack has the same amount.
